comments.author: GreenElm
comments.text:
Why policy is modeled as a first-class dependency surface:

The policy engine and lifecycle specifications are supposed to be the canonical interpreters of gate logic, waiver handling, freshness rules, and compatibility boundaries.

Other runtime or research surfaces should consume these rules, not silently reimplement them. That is why many later beads depend on this epic: without a single governed decision surface, the rest of the protocol becomes difficult to audit and easy to drift.

title: Build the shared policy engine and machine-readable decision traces
description:
## Purpose
Centralize lifecycle transitions, readiness checks, waivers, freshness rules, and promotion decisions in one engine that emits auditable decision traces.

## Plan Detail
### 11.1 Policy engine

All lifecycle transitions, session-readiness checks, waivers, freshness checks, and promotion decisions are evaluated by a shared policy engine.

The engine must emit machine-readable decision traces including:

- policy bundle hash,
- inputs,
- evaluated rules,
- resulting decision,
- and waiver references.

No UI, notebook, or service may reimplement gate logic independently.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- The policy engine evaluates the gate categories listed in the plan.
- Decision traces carry policy bundle hash, inputs, rule evaluation, outcome, and waiver references.
- No other UI or service path needs to silently reimplement gate logic to function.
- Policy rules are covered by deterministic unit and golden-fixture tests that validate both decisions and emitted decision traces.
- Policy failures emit stable reason codes and explanatory diagnostics that downstream tools and operators can consume.

- Completion requires the mapped verification coverage from the shared verification plan: deterministic unit or contract coverage for the rules, schemas, state transitions, or invariants this bead defines; scenario scripts or drills for the workflows those rules govern; and retained detailed logs, correlation IDs, expected-versus-actual diffs, artifact manifests where relevant, and operator-readable reason bundles.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- This is the anti-divergence task for program gating logic.
- Central policy is what makes later audits, promotion packets, and readiness decisions explainable.
- If gate logic is duplicated elsewhere, consistency fails quickly.
- Opaque policy denials are a user-experience bug as well as a governance bug.
close_reason: Completed

title: Publish lifecycle state-machine specs and compatibility domains
description:
## Purpose
Define lifecycle rules and compatibility checks in shared machine-readable specs so Python tools, Rust runtime, SQL checks, CLI tools, and dashboards all speak the same protocol.

## Plan Detail
### 11.2 Lifecycle state machines and compatibility domains

Lifecycle state machines are defined in shared, machine-readable specs and consumed by:

- Python tooling,
- Rust runtime,
- SQL checks,
- CLI tooling,
- and dashboards.

Compatibility is handled by explicit domains rather than one monolithic protocol version. At minimum:

- data protocol,
- strategy protocol,
- ops protocol,
- policy bundle hash,
- and compatibility matrix version.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Lifecycle state machines are represented in shared specs rather than scattered per-service logic.
- Compatibility is checked across the domains listed in the plan rather than compressed into one opaque version number.
- Later startup checks, migrations, and promotion logic can cite these domains directly.
- State-machine transitions and compatibility-domain behavior are exercised through contract fixtures with expected transition logs and failure explanations.

- Completion requires the mapped verification coverage from the shared verification plan: deterministic unit or contract coverage for the rules, schemas, state transitions, or invariants this bead defines; scenario scripts or drills for the workflows those rules govern; and retained detailed logs, correlation IDs, expected-versus-actual diffs, artifact manifests where relevant, and operator-readable reason bundles.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- This is the protocol backbone for cross-plane consistency.
- Compatibility needs domain granularity because not every change affects every part of the stack equally.
- Machine-readable specs keep later tools from inventing their own partial interpretations.
- Lifecycle specs should be easy to test and hard to misread.
close_reason: Completed: published shared lifecycle state-machine catalog, compatibility-domain checks, fixture-driven transition coverage, and verification-plan mapping

title: Implement solo governance, incidents, and waivers as explicit workflows
description:
## Purpose
Make solo operation honest by replacing fake dual control with time-separated self-attestation, signed checklists, explicit waiver expiry, and incident-tracking workflows.

## Plan Detail
### 11.3 Solo governance mode

Because the platform is solo-operated, governance uses signed, time-separated self-attestations instead of fake dual control.

Required controls:

- two attestation steps separated by a cooling-off interval,
- signed checklist artifacts,
- explicit waiver expiry,
- and incident/corrective-action tracking.

### 11.4 Incidents and waivers

Every live-impacting exception requires either:

- a signed waiver with expiry and scope,
- or an incident record with corrective-action closure.

Waivers may not silently become permanent policy.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Cooling-off interval, signed checklist, waiver expiry, and corrective-action requirements are explicit.
- Live-impacting exceptions are captured as waivers or incidents rather than silent operator judgment.
- Waivers cannot drift into permanent untracked policy.
- Solo-governance, waiver, and incident flows are exercised through audit fixtures that preserve the resulting records, expiry behavior, and human-readable rationale.

- Completion requires the mapped verification coverage from the shared verification plan: deterministic unit or contract coverage for the local rules, schemas, calculations, state transitions, or invariants this bead defines; scenario scripts or drills for the workflows those rules govern; and retained detailed logs, correlation IDs, expected-versus-actual diffs, relevant artifact manifests, and operator-readable reason bundles.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- The plan recognizes solo operation as a reality and still asks for real discipline.
- This matters because live safety exceptions will happen and need structure.
- Incidents and waivers should later feed readiness, continuation, and postmortem review.
- Governance workflows should be testable because exceptions are where process usually drifts.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement observability dashboards, alert classes, and operator response targets
description:
## Purpose
Make protocol health, live-lane safety, discovery burn, readiness expiry, recovery evidence, and emergency capability visible enough that the solo operator can act before degraded conditions compound.

## Plan Detail
### 11.5 Observability

The platform must expose metrics and dashboards for:

- release counts and statuses,
- research funnel counts,
- discovery-budget consumption,
- portability, tradability, and replay pass rates,
- account-fit pass rates,
- bar-parity drift,
- session-readiness status and expiry windows,
- live quality events,
- strategy-health drift,
- broker latency and disconnects,
- intraday broker-state mismatch counts,
- guardian health and last emergency drill,
- policy-engine latency and failures,
- active waivers and expiry countdowns,
- backup freshness, journal-digest verification, and restore-drill status,
- clock-synchronization health,
- secret-rotation age and break-glass events,
- and deployment status counts.

The platform must also define alert classes and operator response targets for at least:

- open live position with degraded health,
- failed or stale session-readiness packet,
- intraday broker-state mismatch,
- backup freshness breach,
- restore-drill expiry,
- clock desynchronization,
- break-glass access,
- and inability to execute emergency flatten.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Metrics and dashboards cover the categories listed in the plan.
- Alert classes and response targets are explicit for the named high-risk conditions.
- Observability can support both day-to-day operation and later evidence review.
- Dashboards and alerts include verification and drill freshness, links to retained artifacts, and enough context to move from an alert to the relevant explain surface quickly.

- Completion requires the mapped verification coverage from the shared verification plan: deterministic unit or contract coverage for the rules, schemas, state transitions, or invariants this bead defines; scenario scripts or drills for the workflows those rules govern; and retained detailed logs, correlation IDs, expected-versus-actual diffs, artifact manifests where relevant, and operator-readable reason bundles.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- This is where policy and runtime become visible instead of hidden in logs.
- Observability should help answer whether the lane is safe right now, not only whether something broke.
- Live-capable status depends on being able to see freshness, restore, clock, and guardian health clearly.
- Operator observability should shorten the path from anomaly to explanation.
close_reason: Completed

title: Implement upgrade and migration policy with startup compatibility checks
description:
## Purpose
Treat schema, runtime, snapshot, journal, and policy changes as governed migrations with explicit replay or recertification consequences and a repo structure that keeps those boundaries understandable.

## Plan Detail
### 11.6 Upgrade and migration policy

Schema, runtime, snapshot/journal, and policy changes are versioned migrations, not informal edits.

Rules:

- every migration declares the affected compatibility domains and the required replay or recertification consequences,
- runtime startup performs hard compatibility checks across binary version, database schema, snapshot/journal format, policy bundle hash, and artifact compatibility matrix,
- no migration or runtime upgrade that can affect paper/shadow/live semantics may be deployed during an active session except under incident procedure,
- changing anything that can reinterpret bars, signals, risk behavior, or broker behavior requires deterministic replay recertification and may require new promotion packets,
- forward-only migrations are allowed only when backup/restore evidence proves recoverability,
- and every live-capable upgrade must have a documented rollback or restore path.

### 11.7 Monorepo structure

The monorepo should be organized roughly as:

- `python/research/` — ingestion, releases, orchestration, backtests, tuning, reporting
- `python/bindings/` — Python bindings for canonical Rust kernels
- `rust/kernels/` — canonical signal kernels and shared deterministic compute
- `rust/opsd/` — operational daemon
- `rust/guardian/` — minimal out-of-band emergency control process
- `rust/watchdog/` — supervisor and health automation
- `shared/schemas/` — Pydantic/Serde contracts and manifest definitions
- `shared/policy/` — declarative policy bundles
- `shared/state_machines/` — lifecycle specs
- `shared/fixtures/` — golden sessions, replay fixtures, broker sessions
- `sql/` — migrations and database constraints
- `infra/` — compose/systemd, monitoring, deployment configs
- `docs/runbooks/` — operator procedures and incident playbooks
- `tests/` — integration, property, regression, and conformance suites

---

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Migrations declare affected compatibility domains and required recertification or replay consequences.
- Startup compatibility checks cover the binary, schema, snapshot or journal, policy, and artifact matrix surfaces named in the plan.
- Repo structure remains aligned with the intended plane boundaries instead of mixing research, kernels, runtime, and shared contracts arbitrarily.
- Migration and compatibility checks emit readable compatibility reports and retained rehearsal artifacts rather than only blocking startup with terse errors.

- Completion requires the mapped verification coverage from the shared verification plan: deterministic unit or contract coverage for the local rules, schemas, calculations, state transitions, or invariants this bead defines; scenario scripts or drills for the workflows those rules govern; and retained detailed logs, correlation IDs, expected-versus-actual diffs, relevant artifact manifests, and operator-readable reason bundles.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- Migration control matters because subtle semantic changes can invalidate replay or promotion assumptions.
- Forward-only changes are acceptable only when recoverability is proven rather than assumed.
- Repo structure is included because boundaries influence whether protocol rules remain understandable over time.
- Migration safety is as much about good explanation as it is about correct blocking.
close_reason: Completed: added governed migration declarations, startup compatibility checks, repo-boundary validation, and fixture-driven upgrade policy coverage

title: Phase 0 milestone: foundation and QA gate
description:
## Purpose
Track the foundational bootstrap and quality gate that must exist before the rest of the program can claim protocol discipline.

## Plan Detail
### Phase 0 — foundation and QA (`v1_core_required`)

Build:

- monorepo bootstrap,
- environment locks,
- schema scaffold,
- policy engine scaffold,
- lifecycle state-machine specs,
- compatibility matrix,
- backup/restore scaffold,
- secret-delivery scaffold,
- clock-health probe,
- artifact indexing,
- journal-digest scaffold,
- golden-session test harness,
- property-based invariant harness,

Exit gate:

- empty or toy manifests are reproducible,
- state transitions are logged and queryable,
- startup compatibility checks are enforced,
- backup metadata, digest health, and secret-health probes are visible,
- contract tests pass,
- and the local stack boots and completes a round-trip.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- The build surfaces listed for Phase 0 exist in at least scaffolded form.
- The exit gate conditions for reproducibility, logging, compatibility enforcement, visibility, and round-trip boot are satisfied.
- Downstream phases depend on this milestone instead of bypassing it informally.

title: Phase 9 milestone: continuation-review and scope-governance gate
description:
## Purpose
Track the signed continuation review that must govern any scope expansion or strategic pivot after the first full protocol cycle.

## Plan Detail
### Phase 9 — continuation review (`v1_core_required`)

After the first full protocol cycle, run a signed continuation review.

Allowed outcomes:

- continue as-is,
- narrow scope,
- raise the minimum capital posture,
- change assumptions,
- switch the primary execution symbol,
- remain paper-only,
- add selected `v1_conditional` capabilities,
- or terminate the program.

No scope expansion is allowed without this review.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- The continuation review allows only the outcomes the plan lists.
- No scope expansion is treated as legitimate until this review happens.
- The milestone depends on actual accumulated evidence from the full protocol cycle rather than optimism alone.
- The continuation review explicitly considers accumulated verification evidence, failure-path drill history, and whether diagnostic UX is good enough for sustainable operation.

- This gate or control bead is not complete until the mapped deterministic unit or contract suites, end-to-end or drill scripts, and retained logs or artifact bundles from the shared verification plan support its pass, hold, pivot, continue, narrow, or rejection decision.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- This milestone turns post-v1 ambition into a governed decision rather than drift.
- The project is explicitly allowed to narrow, pivot, remain paper-only, or terminate.
- That honesty is a core feature of the plan, not a failure case.
- Continuation is not only about economics; it is also about whether the stack can be operated and debugged honestly.

title: Build paper-runtime, evidence-capture, and reconciliation scenario test suites
description:
## Purpose
Make paper trading, shadow-live operation, evidence capture, session-readiness gating, operating-envelope enforcement, and broker reconciliation executable in automated test scenarios before live-readiness work begins.

## Scope
This bead covers test suites for Phase 7 surfaces: opsd/guardian/watchdog startup and shutdown, session-readiness packet issuance and validation, operating-envelope band transitions, live bar-builder correctness, order-intent journal idempotency, broker-state reconciliation (intraday and authoritative), evidence archive sealing and queryability, shadow-live non-economic routing, and next-session eligibility gating. Each scenario should preserve timelines, correlated IDs, inputs, outputs, and diagnostic artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Opsd, guardian, and watchdog startup and shutdown sequences are tested with deterministic scenarios that verify state-ownership boundaries and component health.
- Session-readiness packet tests cover green-pass, yellow-degrade, and red-reject scenarios with structured diagnostic reports explaining each gating decision.
- Operating-envelope tests verify green/yellow/red band transitions and the correct action repertoire (restrict, exit-only, flatten, halt) for each band.
- Order-intent idempotency tests prove that duplicate or replayed intents do not create duplicate broker actions.
- Intraday and authoritative broker-state reconciliation tests compare internal ledger against broker statements with structured diff output.
- Evidence archive tests verify sealing, immutability after seal, and queryability of sealed evidence.
- Shadow-live routing tests confirm non-economic order suppression while preserving full decision and signal trace.
- Next-session eligibility tests verify that failed reconciliation or session-readiness correctly blocks the following session.
- All scenarios produce correlated logs, timelines, expected-vs-actual diffs, and operator-readable diagnostic bundles.

- The paper-runtime suites are runnable as end-to-end scenario scripts and are backed by focused unit or contract checks for readiness, routing, evidence sealing, and reconciliation invariants.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- Paper-runtime tests are the last verification gate before real money is at risk.
- Reconciliation tests must cover both normal-path and discrepancy-found paths, since silent reconciliation failures are the highest-risk operational gap.
- Guardian emergency tests should verify independent operation even when opsd is impaired.

title: Build live-readiness, fault-injection, and resilience drill suites
description:
## Purpose
Make the Phase 8 resilience claims executable through automated fault-injection, recovery, and operational-readiness drill suites before live activation is considered.

## Scope
This bead covers test suites for Phase 8 surfaces: fault-injection scenarios, broker-session replay fixtures, idempotent submit/modify/cancel race tests, recovery-fence tests, graceful shutdown/restart tests, restart-while-holding for overnight candidates, contract-conformance and daily-reset tests, guardian emergency drills, clock-drift fail-safe tests, backup/restore drills, journal-digest verification drills, migration rehearsal tests, break-glass and secret-rotation drills, and solo-governance live-approval workflow validation. Each drill should capture timing, state transitions, recovery outcomes, and operator-grade diagnostic artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria

See the structured acceptance criteria field for the canonical checklist.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

- Fault-injection tests cover network partition, broker disconnect, database lock contention, and feed-gap scenarios with verified recovery behavior.
- Broker-session replay tests verify correct state reconstruction after session reset, including position reconciliation.
- Submit/modify/cancel race tests prove that no combination of concurrent operations produces duplicate orders or orphaned intents.
- Recovery-fence tests confirm that the system refuses new entries during recovery and produces diagnostic reasons.
- Graceful shutdown and restart tests verify that no open positions are orphaned and that state is consistent after restart.
- Restart-while-holding tests verify correct behavior for overnight positions across session boundaries.
- Guardian emergency drills verify independent cancel/flatten capability even when opsd is impaired or unresponsive.
- Clock-drift tests verify fail-safe behavior when system clock diverges beyond configured threshold.
- Backup/restore drills verify complete data integrity after restore and measure recovery-point and recovery-time metrics.
- Journal-digest verification drills confirm tamper detection for order-intent journals.
- Migration rehearsal tests verify that schema or configuration upgrades can be applied and rolled back without data loss.
- Break-glass and secret-rotation drills verify credential access, usage logging, and post-use rotation.
- Solo-governance live-approval tests verify cooling-off, signed-checklist, and waiver workflows.
- All drills produce structured timing, state-transition, recovery-outcome, and operator-grade diagnostic logs.

- The resilience suite combines failure-path end-to-end drill scripts with focused unit or contract checks on safety invariants, state transitions, and explicit safe-outcome assertions.
notes:
## Elaboration and Guardrails

- Phase 8 is the final safety gate before live money is deployed.
- These drills must be runnable repeatedly without side effects on real infrastructure.
- Guardian emergency drills are the single most important resilience test — guardian must work even when everything else is broken.
- Fault-injection scenarios should be composable so multiple failure modes can be combined.
